coredata.dc:title: Exploring the next super fluorescent carbon Dots: Hydrothermal synthesis of Dual-Function ultra-bright blue fluorescent carbon dots with a quantum yield of 75.4%
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Xiang
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Low quantum yield is a prevalent limitation of most fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), significantly impeding their broader applications. In this study, we present the synthesis of a novel type of ultra-bright fluorescent carbon dots (UB-CDs) derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine. Employing a one-step hydrothermal method with malonic acid as the carbon source and ammonium acetate as the nitrogen source, we successfully synthesized UB-CDs exhibiting a high quantum yield of 75.4 %. Capitalizing on the selective response of UB-CDs to Hg2+ ions, we developed a fluorescent probe specifically designed for the detection of mercury ions in environmental samples. Additionally, leveraging the superior fluorescent properties of UB-CDs, we formulated a CDs-based fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting application. This research offers a straightforward and effective strategy for synthesizing high quantum yield CDs, thereby enhancing their potential in analytical detection and innovative display technologies. Furthermore, it addresses the pervasive challenge of low quantum yield, which has traditionally constrained the widespread application of fluorescent CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High quantum yield
Fluorescent sensor
Fluorescent ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application 2: Anti-counterfeiting ink for information encryption

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of UB-CDs.

The structure characterization of UB-CDs. (a) TEM image. The upper left inset is HRTEM; (b) Infrared spectroscopy; (c) XPS survey spectrum; (d-f) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s elements, respectively.

Optical properties of UB-CDs. (a) Photos of UB-CDs under natural light (left) and ultraviolet light (right). (b) Two-dimensional projection of the excitation-emission matrix. (c) Ultraviolet–visible absorption (purple), excitation (blue), and emission spectra (polychromatic). (d) CIE chromaticity diagram. (e) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (f) quantum yield comparisons of UB-CDs in different solvents.

Application of UB-CDs as chemical sensor for Hg2+ (a) The selectivity of UB CDs solution towards metal ions (b) The effect of different concentrations of Hg2+(0–240 μM) on the fluorescence intensity of UB-CDs (c) Linear fitting between Hg2+ concentration and UB-CDs fluorescence intensity (d) UV spectra of UB-CDs, Hg2+, and their complexes with Hg2+ (e) UV absorption spectra of Hg2+ and excitation/emission spectra of UB-CDs.

Application of UB-CDs as anti-counterfeiting ink with encrypted information. (a) Anti-counterfeiting two-dimensional code made of UB-CDs under natural light and ultraviolet light. (b) Anti-counterfeiting two-dimensional code changes over time.
Detection of Hg2+ by different probes.
Hg2+ detection in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Exploring the next super fluorescent carbon Dots: Hydrothermal synthesis of Dual-Function ultra-bright blue fluorescent carbon dots with a quantum yield of 75.4%

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low quantum yield is a prevalent limitation of most fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), significantly impeding their broader applications. In this study, we present the synthesis of a novel type of ultra-bright fluorescent carbon dots (UB-CDs) derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine. Employing a one-step hydrothermal method with malonic acid as the carbon source and ammonium acetate as the nitrogen source, we successfully synthesized UB-CDs exhibiting a high quantum yield of 75.4 %. Capitalizing on the selective response of UB-CDs to Hg2+ ions, we developed a fluorescent probe specifically designed for the detection of mercury ions in environmental samples. Additionally, leveraging the superior fluorescent properties of UB-CDs, we formulated a CDs-based fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting application. This research offers a straightforward and effective strategy for synthesizing high quantum yield CDs, thereby enhancing their potential in analytical detection and innovative display technologies. Furthermore, it addresses the pervasive challenge of low quantum yield, which has traditionally constrained the widespread application of fluorescent CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials characterized by dimensions of less than 10 nm [1]. These materials offer several advantages, including low toxicity, straightforward synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, and robust chemical and photostability [2–4]. As a result of these properties, CDs are extensively utilized in analytical detection [5], cellular imaging [6], and next-generation display technologies [7]. However, despite their significant potential, many traditional fluorescent materials are hindered in their applications due to various limitations. For example, semiconductor quantum dots demonstrate high quantum yield but are associated with considerable toxicity, which restricts their range of applications. In contrast, although many CDs exhibit lower toxicity, their quantum yields are frequently inadequate for practical use. Specifically, CDs synthesized by Nguyen et al. reported a quantum yield of only 9.6% [8], while Shi et al. achieved a yield of 5% [9], and Lee et al. attained merely 2.5% [10].

While several synthesis methods for producing high quantum yield CDs have been documented, these often encounter challenges such as poor stability or intricate production processes. For instance, Zhao et al. synthesized CDs with a quantum yield of 40% using citric acid and urea [11]; however, the stability of these CDs was suboptimal, complicating their storage. Similarly, Chen et al. developed green fluorescent CDs with an impressive quantum yield of 80% [12], but the complexity of their synthesis procedures hinders widespread adoption. Moreover, Liu et al. reported the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots with a quantum yield approaching 100% using phthalic acid [13], while Qu et al. achieved a quantum yield of 90% through microwave pyrolysis [14]. Nonetheless, reports of high quantum yield CDs remain relatively scarce. Therefore, developing a simple and efficient method for producing stable CDs with high quantum yield holds significant practical importance. However, the contentious nature of the luminescence mechanism associated with CDs presents considerable challenges for their controlled synthesis.

Hg2+ is one of the most prevalent and hazardous heavy metal ions, representing a significant environmental pollutant. It can migrate through water, air, and soil, subsequently entering the human body via the food chain, where it accumulates in critical organs such as the liver and kidneys, thereby posing considerable health risks [15,16]. Currently, the primary methods for detecting Hg2+ include atomic fluorescence spectrometry [17], atomic emission spectrometry [18], and atomic absorption spectrometry [19]. While these techniques provide high sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ detection, they are often costly, complex to operate, and unsuitable for on-site, real-time testing [20,21]. In contrast, fluorescence analysis presents several advantages, including non-destructive sample handling, ease of operation, low detection limits, minimal sample volume requirements, and reliance on relatively simple equipment while maintaining good selectivity. These attributes have facilitated the widespread adoption of fluorescence analysis in environmental monitoring. In recent years, fluorescent probes have emerged as increasingly important reagents in fluorescence analysis, with CDs being extensively utilized for the qualitative and quantitative detection of Hg2+ in environmental samples [22]. However, most CDs currently employed as fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection exhibit low quantum yield, poor selectivity, and high detection limits. For instance, Correia et al. synthesized Eu (III)-doped CDs using citric acid, urea, and Eu(NO3)3, achieving a quantum yield of only 1.3% [23]. The fluorescent probe developed from these CDs demonstrated a detection limit significantly higher than that of other probes in the same category. Similarly, Ahmed et al. reported N-doped CDs with a quantum yield of 24.8% [24]. Although the detection limit was superior to that of some similar fluorescent probes, the N-doped CDs exhibited responses to not only Hg2+ but also Fe3+ and chloramphenicol (CAP), indicating a lack of selectivity. Studies have demonstrated that enhancing the quantum yield of CDs can improve their selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ detection [25–27]. Therefore, the development of high quantum yield fluorescent CDs for Hg2+ detection holds significant practical value.

In this study, ultra-bright blue fluorescent carbon dots (UB-CDs) were synthesized using malonic acid and ammonium acetate as precursors through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Under 365 nm UV light, these UB-CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of up to 75.4%. The UB-CDs were utilized as fluorescent quenching probes for the detection of Hg2+ in water, achieving a detection limit of 2.7 μM and a detection range of 0–240 μM. Additionally, leveraging the strong luminescence properties of the CDs, fluorescent QR codes were fabricated for information anti-counterfeiting purposes. This work presents a straightforward strategy for synthesizing high quantum yield carbon dots, thereby broadening their applications in analytical detection and next-generation display technologies.

All chemical reagents and solvents, including malonic acid (MA), ammonium acetate, ethanol (EtOH), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (ACE), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and sodium chloride were supplied by Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Metal ion samples (Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, etc.) were obtained from the National Analysis Center for Nonferrous Metals and Electronic Materials. All aqueous solutions used in this study were prepared with double-distilled water produced in our laboratory.

The high-pressure reactor (25 mL) was acquired from Xiniou Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaner was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd., and the electric constant temperature forced air drying oven was obtained from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shunyu Hengping 756PC UV–visible spectrophotometer, while fluorescence spectra were acquired with a Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

In this study, UB-CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method with malonic acid and ammonium acetate as precursors. The synthesis procedure was as follows: 0.385 g of malonic acid and 0.5 g of ammonium acetate were accurately weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water. The solution was then sonicated for 10 min in an ultrasonic cleaner to ensure thorough mixing and uniform dispersion of the reactants. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a high-pressure reactor and placed in a preheated oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the reactor was removed from the oven and allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particulate impurities. Finally, the supernatant was purified through dialysis to obtain the target UB-CDs. The detailed synthesis process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The photoluminescence quantum yield of the synthesized UB-CDs was determined using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, which has a known quantum yield of 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The measurement procedure was as follows: A series of diluted solutions of both quinine sulfate and the UB-CDs sample were prepared, ensuring that their absorbance values at the maximum excitation wavelength did not exceed 0.05. Absorption and fluorescence spectra were then recorded for these solutions at various concentrations. The quantum yield was calculated based on the following equation:
(1)
φ U
=
φ S
·
F U
F S
·
A S
A U
·
(
η S
η U
)
2
=
φ S
·
k U
k S
·
(
η S
η U
)
2

Where φ represents the quantum yield of the CDs, quantifying their ability to convert absorbed light energy into fluorescence; F refers to the integrated fluorescence signal from the emission spectrum of the CDs measured in UV spectral analysis; A denotes the absorbance of the CDs at a specific excitation wavelength measured in UV–visible spectral analysis; k represents the slope obtained from the fitted curve of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance; η is the refractive index of the solvent used in the synthesis of the CDs, with subscripts S and U denoting the reference standard and the actual sample under test, respectively.

The structural and chemical composition of UB-CDs were comprehensively analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). TEM image (Fig. 2 a) reveals that UB-CDs exhibit an irregular granular morphology with an average particle size of approximately 2 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene [28]. FT-IR was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of the UB-CDs in detail, with the results presented in Fig. 2b. The characteristic peak observed at 846 cm−1 is attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the N-H bond in amine groups [29]; the absorption peak at 1012 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond in amide groups [30]. The absorption peaks at 1126 cm−1, 1583 cm−1, and 1718 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of the C-O-C bond in ester molecules, the antisymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate ions (–COO−), and the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups (C=O), respectively [31,32]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 2852 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of methyl groups (–CH2) [33], and the peaks at 2943 cm−1, 3059 cm−1, 3317 cm−1, 3645 cm−1, and 3780 cm−1 are likely due to C-H, N-H, and –OH groups [34,35]. Collectively, these results indicate that the UB-CDs synthesized in this study contain functional groups such as –NH2, −C=O, and –OH, which confer excellent hydrophilicity to the UB-CDs and confirm successful N-doping, forming N-doped CDs. To further investigate the surface composition of the UB-CDs, XPS analysis was conducted. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2c) displayed characteristic peaks for O-1s, N-1s, and C-1s at 532 eV, 401 eV, and 286 eV, respectively, confirming that UB-CDs mainly consist of C, N, and O elements. Atomic ratio analysis revealed the atomic percentages of C, N, and O to be 58.15%, 13.35%, and 28.5%, further validating the successful incorporation of nitrogen. High-resolution XPS spectra of C-1s, N-1s, and O-1s were deconvoluted to provide a more detailed understanding of the surface chemical states of UB-CDs. The C-1s peak (Fig. 2d) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.0 eV (C-N/C-O), and 288.0 eV (C=O) [36]. The O-1s peaks, located at 533.32 eV (O=C) and 531.89 eV (O-C) [37], are shown in Fig. 2e. The N-1s deconvoluted peaks correspond to graphitic N (400.2 eV) and pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) [38], as illustrated in Fig. 2f. These findings reveal the surface chemical structure of UB-CDs, providing essential insights into their properties and potential applications.

The optical properties of UB-CDs were thoroughly examined using excitation-emission matrix, UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, excitation spectra, and emission spectra. Fig. 3 a shows photos of a dilute solution of UB-CDs under natural light and ultraviolet light. It is evident that UB-CDs are almost colorless and transparent, and emit strong blue light under ultraviolet light. Fig. 3b shows the two-dimensional projection of the excitation-emission matrix (EEM), clearly revealing the distribution of carbon dots across both excitation and emission regions. The excitation peaks are mainly concentrated in the 260–390 nm wavelength range, while the emission peaks are observed between 400 and 500 nm. In Fig. 3c, the UV–visible spectra of UB-CDs, including both excitation and emission spectra, are depicted. The analysis shows three absorption peaks at 210 nm, 269 nm, and 310 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions of C=C at 210 nm and n-π* transitions of C=O or C=N at 269 nm and 310 nm, respectively [39]. These absorptions are likely linked to the fluorescent emission process, suggesting that these surface functional groups contribute to the emission centers of the UB-CDs. The excitation and emission spectra further reveal that the maximum excitation wavelength for the UB-CDs is 369 nm, with a corresponding maximum emission wavelength at 446 nm. The emission spectrum, filled with color beneath the curve, indicates that the emitted fluorescence at the peak emission wavelength is blue, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 78 nm. The minimal overlap between excitation and emission spectra suggests a weak interaction between the two. Additionally, the CIE chromaticity diagram in Fig. 3d confirms the blue fluorescence emitted by the UB-CDs.

Fig. 3e displays the emission spectra of UB-CDs under different excitation wavelengths from 350 nm to 400 nm. It is clear that the UB-CDs show excitation-independent emissions, suggesting that UB-CDs may have uniform fluorescent chromophore and their luminescence behavior is not significantly affected by quantum size effects [40]. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, one can estimate the quantum yields of UB-CDs in various solvents based on Eq. (1), with the results presented in Fig. 3f. Water shows the highest quantum yield at approximately 75.4%, followed by EtOH at 28.39%, while other solvents (DMSO, THF, DMF, ACE) have lower quantum yields in the range of 10–20%. These results demonstrate that UB-CDs exhibit optimal photoluminescent properties in water, including the strongest emission intensity, longest emission wavelength, and highest quantum yield. The performance in protic solvents (water and EtOH) is notably better than in aprotic solvents, likely due to differences in microstructure and intermolecular interactions.

Under ambient conditions, we systematically investigated the influence of various metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of ultra-bright carbon dots (UB-CDs), with results visually represented in Fig. 4 d. The presence of metal ions such as Pb2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, and Sn2+ did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of UB-CDs. In contrast, ions such as Cu2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, and Cd2+ resulted in a slight decrease in fluorescence, although this effect was negligible. Notably, Hg2+ exhibited a pronounced reduction in fluorescence intensity, demonstrating the strong selectivity of UB-CDs for Hg2+. To optimize the detection conditions for Hg2+, we systematically varied the quantity of UB-CDs, solution pH, and reaction duration. The results, depicted in Fig. 4a-c, show that the fluorescence intensity before (I 0) and after (I) the addition of Hg2+ (0.2 mM) was significantly influenced by the amount of UB-CDs used. A maximum fluorescence intensity was observed with 100 µL of UB-CDs, indicating this as the optimal condition for subsequent experiments. The impact of solution pH on detection results is illustrated in Fig. 4b, revealing that pH significantly affects the detection signal, with an optimal pH of 7 identified for future experiments. Fig. 4c demonstrates that the reaction time did not lead to significant changes in detection signals, enabling rapid detection of Hg2+ by UB-CDs.

To assess the sensitivity of UB-CDs to Hg2+, we incrementally increased the Hg2+ concentration in the solution under controlled conditions. As shown in Fig. 4e, fluorescence intensity consistently decreased with rising Hg2+ concentrations. A linear regression analysis across a concentration range of 0 to 240 µM yielded the equation (I 0-I)/I=0.0025C-0.0232, with an R 2 value of 0.9945, indicating a strong linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration (Fig. 4f). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 2.7 µM, highlighting the efficacy of UB-CDs as a rapid and precise fluorescent indicator for Hg2+. These results suggest that the newly synthesized fluorescent probe, UB-CDs, can achieve accurate and precise label-free detection of Hg2+ in environmental water samples. Table 1 compares the detection range and LOD of the UB-CDs developed in this study with other reported fluorescent probes, demonstrating superior performance in both aspects.

Analysis of UV–visible spectra before and after the addition of Hg2+ revealed absorption peaks at 220 nm and 307 nm, as shown in Fig. 4g. The intensity of the UB-CDs absorption peak at 210 nm significantly decreased upon the addition of Hg2+, indicating that functional groups on the carbon dots’ surface (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) may form stable complexes with Hg2+, which lack fluorescent properties, thereby causing fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, fluorescence lifetime measurements before and after Hg2+ addition showed no significant changes (Fig. 4i), further corroborating that the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots results from the formation of a non-fluorescent complex, indicating static quenching. Fig. 4h illustrates a significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ and the excitation spectrum of UB-CDs. This suggests that when Hg2+ interacts with the carbon dots, it may absorb some excitation or emission light, leading to a reduction in the detected fluorescence signal and contributing to the observed fluorescence quenching. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of UB-CDs in the presence of Hg2+ is attributed to a combination of the inner filter effect and static quenching.

To evaluate the practical application of this method, tap water was used as a real-world sample. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates of Hg2+ ranged from 93.2% to 98.86%, with relative standard deviations (RSD) under 2.5%. These results demonstrate the potential of UB-CDs as a fluorescent probe for detecting Hg2+ in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application 2: Anti-counterfeiting ink for information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excellent fluorescence stability of UB-CDs, they can be used as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink for information encryption. Fig. 5 a illustrates a QR code made with UB-CDs, which is invisible under natural light but reveals hidden information under UV light, ensuring information concealment and encryption. Fig. 5b shows the anti-counterfeiting effect over time, where a slight decrease in brightness is observed, but the pattern remains clearly visible even after three days, demonstrating the durability of UB-CDs. This technology can be applied to scenarios requiring short-term anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, such as important documents or product packaging, offering an innovative method for enhanced security.

In this study, we successfully synthesized high-performance blue fluorescent carbon dots using malonic acid and ammonium acetate as precursors in an aqueous solvent through a hydrothermal method. The UB-CDs exhibited maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 369 nm and 446 nm, respectively, demonstrating excitation-independent emissions behavior. The quantum yield of UB-CDs reaches up to 75.4% in protic solvents while being relatively lower in aprotic solvents. Importantly, a strong linear relationship (correlation coefficient of 0.9945) was observed between the fluorescence intensity of UB-CDs and Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 0–240 μM. This finding enabled the development of a rapid and effective fluorescence-based analytical method for Hg2+ detection, which has significant implications for real-sample analysis. Furthermore, leveraging the excellent fluorescent properties of UB-CDs, we developed CDs-based fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting applications. In summary, this work presents a facile strategy for preparing CDs with high quantum yields, expanding their potential applications in analytical detection and novel display technologies.
Xiang Long: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
